mnemonics.title: DISTRIBUTED SYSTEM CHARACTERISTICS
mnemonics.content: TRACS: TRANSPARENCY, RELIABILITY, AVAILABILITY, CONSISTENCY, SCALABILITY - These five form the foundation of good distributed system design.

mnemonics.title: CAP THEOREM
mnemonics.content: CAP: CONSISTENCY, AVAILABILITY, PARTITION TOLERANCE - Pick any two! When network partitions happen, you must choose between consistency and availability.

mnemonics.title: DISTRIBUTED SYSTEM CHALLENGES
mnemonics.content: HANDLES: HETEROGENEOUS components, ACCESS security, NETWORK issues, DELAYS, HORIZONTAL scaling, ENTROPY (disorder), SYNCHRONIZATION problems, Transparency gaps.

mnemonics.title: RPC MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: REMOTE PROCEDURE CALL: Think of it like ordering food at a restaurant - you (client) send request to kitchen (server), food (result) comes back, you dont know the kitchen details!

acronyms.usage: Used to execute code on remote machines as if calling a local function
Java-specific RPC for calling methods on remote objects
Architecture where all nodes are equal and can be both clients and servers
Distributed database that translates domain names to IP addresses
Fundamental theorem describing tradeoffs in distributed systems
Reference model for network communication in distributed systems
Network connecting computers in a limited area for distributed computing

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a restaurant: Customers (clients) sit at tables and place orders, while the Kitchen (server) prepares food and serves it back. The waiter acts as the network middleware.
Think of a potluck dinner where everyone brings a dish and shares with everyone else. There is no central kitchen - each person is both a contributor and consumer.

Picture an airport international terminal - different airlines (applications) and different countries (operating systems) communicate through the terminal building (middleware) that handles translation and coordination.
Imagine a library system where book copies exist in multiple branches. When you borrow a book, the system updates all branches so everyone sees the same availability status.
Think of sending a letter versus making a local phone call. Local procedure call is like talking face-to-face (instant); RPC is like mailing a letter (delayed response).

analogies.analogy: Centralized is like one person doing all the work in an office; Distributed is like a team where everyone works on their part but coordinates to complete the project together.
Like an automatic transmission in a car - the driver doesnt need to know about gears, clutches, or how the engine connects to wheels; it all works seamlessly.
Like having multiple exit routes in a building - if one door is blocked, people can still exit through other doors without knowing which one might fail.
Like expanding a restaurant - vertical scaling is adding more floors to the building; horizontal scaling is opening more branches across the city.
Like two friends planning a trip with only phone calls - if one changes plans and the other doesnt know, they end up at different locations. Distributed systems face this constantly.
